Short Description: Aalborg Portland Carbon Capture and Storage using Infrastructure Onshore in North Jutland
Full Description: ACCSION is a large-scale CCS project at Aalborg Portland’s cement plant in Aalborg, Denmark, developed with Air Liquide to establish an integrated onshore carbon capture, transport, and storage chain. It captures CO? from both grey and white cement production using Cryocap™ technology and transports it by pipeline to onshore storage in Denmark. The project will capture about 1.4–1.42 MtCO? per year and avoid around 1.5 MtCO? annually, including benefits from surplus heat recovery. Around 80 MW of excess heat will be supplied to district heating. The project aims to enable near net-zero scope 1 emissions and demonstrate a scalable CCS model for heavy industry.
Technology Type:
Capture: Air Liquide Cryocap™ cryogenic CO? capture system, capturing CO? from flue-gas streams in a single integrated setup.
Transport: Pipeline transport from the Aalborg cement site to a CO? reception/transport infrastructure.
Storage: Onshore geological storage in Denmark / onshore CO? storage facilities (exact final storage formation/site not confirmed).
Integration notes: renewable electricity supply for the capture plant, certified biogas use in kilns, digital monitoring, and recovery of ~80 MW surplus heat for district heating.

Full Description: AdriatiCO? captures CO? from two emission points at Marcegaglia Ravenna steel plant: a gas-fired cogeneration unit (biomethane-fuelled) and a green DRI facility (biochar-based) — the first commercial biochar-DRI application globally. A patented post-combustion novel-solvent system with heat recovery captures up to 112 kt CO?/yr (>40% of site Scope 1 emissions). Biogenic fuel substitution at both points enables BECCS, making this the first such deployment in Italy and Southern Europe. Captured CO? is transported via Eni/Snam pipeline to the Ravenna offshore geological storage hub (Adriatic Sea)
Technology Type:
Post-combustion capture (novel solvent / patented process) + BECCS
Specifically: post-combustion solvent-based absorption using a patented novel solvent combined with an integrated heat recovery system, applied to flue gases from a gas-fired cogeneration unit and a green DRI facility. Biogenic fuel substitution (biomethane in cogeneration; biochar in DRI) enables the BECCS classification. CO? transport via pipeline; offshore geological storage in depleted Adriatic reservoirs via the Ravenna CCS hub (Eni/Snam).

Address: San Martin 5, Edificio Muńatones, 48550 San Julián de Musques o Somorrostro, Biscay, Spain
Facility Type: CO? mineralisation plant linked to refinery CO? source
Short Description: Fabrication of CO2 negative AGGREGAtes based on disruptive accelerated carbonation processes fuelled by carbon capture in refineries
Full Description: AGGREGACO2 is a small-scale Innovation Fund project in Spain led by Biscay Eco Aggregates, producing carbon-negative aggregates through CO? mineralisation. The project uses CO? from the Petronor refinery and reacts it with residues from Energy-from-Waste plants using Accelerated Carbonation Technology. This process converts waste materials into construction aggregates while permanently storing CO? as solid carbonates. The facility will process around 22,000 tonnes of residues annually and produce about 56,000 tonnes of aggregates. The project demonstrates circular carbon use by combining industrial CO? utilisation with waste valorisation in the construction sector.
Technology Type: CO? is sourced externally from the Petronor refinery (Steam Methane Reformer flue gas) with no on-site capture, and is transferred over a short distance to the mineralisation facility, where Accelerated Carbonation Technology (ACT, O.C.O Technology) is applied to react CO? with air pollution control residues (APCr) from energy-from-waste processes, producing construction aggregates while permanently storing CO? as solid carbonates; the system operates at a pilot scale of approximately 2.2 ktCO? per year.

Address: Rue de la Cimenterie, 79600 Airvault, France
Short Description: First large-scale CCUS network for deep inland emitters starting from the Europe’s first zero water intake cement plant
Full Description: AirvaultGOCO? is a large-scale CCUS project at Heidelberg Materials’ cement plant in Airvault, France, aiming to capture approximately 1 MtCO? per year. The project is part of the GOCO? initiative to decarbonise industrial sites in western France by establishing a full CO? transport and storage value chain. Captured CO? will be transported by pipeline to Saint-Nazaire and shipped to offshore geological storage in the North Sea. A portion of biogenic CO? may also be used for e-fuel production. The project is expected to start capturing CO? around 2030, subject to funding and regulatory approvals.
Technology Type: Post-combustion CO? capture at a cement plant (~1 MtCO?/year planned), with CO? transported via pipeline to Saint-Nazaire and then shipped to offshore geological storage in the North Sea; part of the CO? stream (biogenic fraction) may be utilised for e-fuel production; final capture technology configuration not yet publicly confirmed.

Coordinator: BZK ENERGY SPOLKA Z OGRANICZONA ODPOWIEDZIALNOSCIA
Site Owner: BZK ENERGY SPOLKA Z OGRANICZONA ODPOWIEDZIALNOSCIA
Short Description: Closed-loop installation for sustAinable production of bioMethane, BioCO2 And biofertilizerS a new StandArD fOr zeRo waste economy
Full Description: AMBASSADOR is a medium-scale Innovation Fund project in Poland led by BZK Energy, developing a closed-loop biogas facility for the production of biomethane, purified CO?, and biofertilisers. The project integrates anaerobic digestion of agricultural and food waste with gas upgrading and CO? capture and liquefaction technologies. Produced biomethane will be injected into the national gas grid, while part of it will be used on-site for energy self-sufficiency. The captured biogenic CO? will be purified and reused, contributing to emissions reduction and circular carbon use. Over its first five years of operation, the project is expected to avoid approximately 128 ktCO?.
Technology Type: Closed-loop biogas system with anaerobic digestion of agricultural and food waste, followed by biomethane upgrading (gas purification) and integrated CO? capture and liquefaction; biomethane is injected into the gas grid and partially used on-site for energy, while captured biogenic CO? is purified for reuse; no geological storage involved (BECCU system).

Short Description: ANRAV will establish Eastern Europe’s first full CCS chain, capturing CO? from Devnya cement plant and storing it offshore in the Black Sea, achieving ~95% emission reduction.
Full Description: ANRAV is a large-scale CCUS project at the Devnya cement plant in Bulgaria, led by Heidelberg Materials, aiming to capture around 0.8 MtCO? per year. The project will establish the first full CCS value chain in Eastern Europe, linking cement production to offshore geological storage in the Black Sea via pipeline infrastructure. Captured CO? will be transported to the depleted Galata gas field for permanent storage, while enabling the development of a regional CCUS cluster.
Technology Type: Hybrid staged CO? capture system combining oxyfuel combustion (OxyCal) and post-combustion amine scrubbing. Achieving up to ~99% capture efficiency; CO? is processed via a CO? Processing Unit (CPU), transported by pipeline, and permanently stored in the depleted Galata gas field under the Black Sea.

Address: Heidelberg Materials Antoing plant, 7640 Antoing, Belgium
Short Description: Antoing Heidelberg Materials Emissions Integrated System
Full Description: ANTHEMIS is a large-scale CCS project at Heidelberg Materials’ Antoing cement plant that will deploy a hybrid oxyfuel and amine carbon capture system to capture over 95% of emissions (~800,000 tCO?/year). The project includes transport and permanent offshore storage, enabling near net-zero cement production from 2029. Invited to IF24 grant agreement preparation (Nov 2025). Amount not confirmed. Permit application submitted H1 2025 for Antoing clinker plant. FID targeted 2026-2027. Operational: 2029.
Technology Type: Oxyfuel-based CO? capture using pure oxygen combustion combined with CO? recycling to produce a high-concentration CO? stream (>80%), followed by cryogenic purification via a CO? Purification Unit (CPU) supplied by Linde to ~98% purity; CO? is then transported via pipeline infrastructure operated by Fluxys and connected to the CO? Highway Europe system, with offshore geological storage in Norway under development in partnership with Equinor; system designed to capture over 800,000 tCO?/year.

Short Description: Creation of an innovative and cost-efficient CO2 management solution to heavy industrial emitters through a Liquefaction Export Terminal, leveraging on the CCS hub approach.
Full Description: APOLLOCO2-LT is a large-scale CO? infrastructure project in Greece, centred at the Revythoussa LNG terminal and developed to serve as a regional CCS hub. The project aims to aggregate CO? from multiple industrial emitters, liquefy it at a dedicated export terminal, and transport it by ship to offshore geological storage sites. It is designed as an open-access system to enable cost-effective CO? management for industries across Southeastern Europe. The project leverages existing gas infrastructure and supports the development of a regional CO? transport and storage value chain. It has been selected for Innovation Fund 2024 grant agreement preparation.
Technology Type: CO? liquefaction export terminal + open-access CCS hub: receives gaseous CO? via pipeline from industrial emitters; liquefies using LNG regasification cold energy; CO? loaded to ships; South-Eastern European CCS infrastructure hub (DESFA/Hellenic Gas operator)

Short Description: Bio-Energy Carbon Capture and Storage (BECCS) at the existing Combined Heat and Power-plant KVV8 at Värtaverket, Stockholm, Sweden
Full Description: BECCS Stockholm is a large-scale carbon removal project developed by Stockholm Exergi at the Värtaverket biomass-fired CHP plant in Sweden. The project will capture approximately 800,000 tonnes of biogenic CO? per year using post-combustion carbon capture technology. The captured CO? will be liquefied and transported by ship to offshore geological storage sites for permanent sequestration. As the CO? originates from biomass, the project delivers negative emissions and contributes to long-term carbon removal. It is one of Europe’s flagship BECCS projects and has received funding from the Innovation Fund 2020.
Technology Type: BECCS: Post-combustion CO? capture (Capsol-based / potassium carbonate / amine hybrid configuration under development) on biomass-fired CHP plant (KVV8, Värtaverket); 100% biogenic CO?; ship transport to offshore North Sea geological storage; negative emission carbon removal; 0.78 Mt/yr

Address: Store Nerheim, 5580 Řlen, Vindafjord, Rogaland, Norway
Facility Type: Biogas/biomethane and bio-LNG plant with bio-CO? recovery and biofertiliser production from organic waste
Short Description: Advancing Sustainable Bio- LNG Production and Waste Valorization
Full Description: BIOVIND is an Innovation Fund IF24 medium-scale project developed by Biovind AS in Vindafjord, Rogaland, Norway. The project is a plant for treating organic waste from nearby farms and food industries to produce biomethane/bio-LNG, biofertiliser and a reusable biogenic CO? stream. Public permit documents describe capacity for up to 226,000 tonnes of organic input per year, biomethane production of about 109 GWh per year, and a separated CO? stream of 7,665 tonnes per year.
Technology Type: Organic waste streams including manure, fish sludge and food waste are treated via anaerobic digestion to produce biogas, which is upgraded to biomethane and liquefied into Bio-LNG. The process separates a biogenic CO? stream of approximately 7.7 kt/year, which is recovered and transported for industrial utilisation. Digestate from the process is hygienised and processed into liquid biofertiliser and solid biorest. The facility is designed for around 226 kt/year of feedstock input and biomethane production of about 9.9 million Nmł/year.

Short Description: Carbon2Business
Full Description: C2B (Carbon2Business) is a large-scale Innovation Fund project developed by Holcim at the Lägerdorf cement plant in Germany. The project captures approximately 1.2–1.3 Mt CO? per year using oxyfuel combustion and a cryogenic carbon processing unit. The captured CO? is supplied to downstream industrial users for conversion into chemicals such as methanol or for plastic production.
Technology Type: Oxyfuel combustion + Carbon Processing Unit (CPU) CO? capture at Heidelberg Materials Lägerdorf cement plant; CO? utilised in thyssenkrupp/Linde CPU to produce industrial chemicals (methanol, plastics precursors) — CCU/conversion utilisation; ~1.3 Mt/yr

Short Description: First industrial scale carbon capture for lime production integrated with transport of CO2 to coastal hub and shipping to geological storage in the North Sea
Full Description: CalCC is a large-scale CCS project at Lhoist’s lime production plant in Réty, France, aiming to capture approximately 600,000 tonnes of CO? per year using Air Liquide’s Cryocap™ FG cryogenic capture technology. The project will integrate a full CCS value chain, with captured CO? transported to a regional CO? hub for liquefaction and shipment to offshore geological storage sites in the North Sea. It represents the first industrial-scale carbon capture application in the lime sector and is part of a broader regional decarbonisation initiative.
Technology Type: Cryogenic CO? capture using Air Liquide’s Cryocap™ FG technology applied to lime kiln flue gases (~0.6 MtCO?/year), followed by transport to a regional CO? hub, liquefaction, ship transport, and offshore geological storage in the North Sea.

Category: Enzymatic carbon capture technology at a Waste-to-Energy plant for offshore CO2 storage
Short Description: Carbon Capture to Decarbonize Herambiente Ferrara Waste-to-Energy Plant
Full Description: CapturEste is a first-of-a-kind CCS project in Italy applied to a waste-to-energy (WtE) plant, located in Ferrara and developed by Herambiente (Hera Group) with Saipem. It aims to decarbonise WtE operations by capturing CO? emissions and linking them to a developing Italian CCS infrastructure (Ravenna hub). The project is positioned as a key step in scaling CCS in the waste sector and enabling a circular low-carbon energy system
Technology Type: Saipem Bluenzyme™ enzymatic CO? capture (biocatalyst enzyme technology) at Herambiente Ferrara Waste-to-Energy plant. CO? transported via pipeline to depleted gas fields in the Adriatic Sea. Italy's first industrial-scale CCS applied to a WtE plant. 64 kt/yr; 90% capture efficiency.

Address: Route de la Garonne, 31220 Martres-Tolosane, France
Full Description: CarboClearTech is a large-scale CCS project at Holcim’s Martres-Tolosane cement plant in France, designed to significantly reduce CO? emissions from clinker production through an integrated carbon capture, transport, and storage chain. Developed in collaboration with Air Liquide and regional infrastructure partners, the project will capture approximately 0.7 MtCO? per year and permanently store it in geological formations in southwestern France. In addition to decarbonising the plant, CarboClearTech serves as an industrial-scale demonstration platform for modular and replicable CCS solutions in the cement sector. The project aims to support Holcim’s net-zero strategy while enabling standardised, scalable CCS deployment across European heavy industry.
Technology Type: VPSA + cryogenic capture. Open innovation test bench for multiple capture technologies from 2025. CO? pipeline to Pyrenean/Lacq geological storage via Teréga infrastructure. 700 kt/yr.

Address: Valea Mare Prav??, Arge? County, Romania
Short Description: CARBON HUB CPT01
Full Description: CARBON HUB CPT01 is a large-scale Innovation Fund project developed by Holcim in Câmpulung, Romania, in partnership with Carmeuse. The project captures CO? emissions from both a cement plant and a neighbouring lime facility through a shared capture system. The captured CO? is compressed and transported via pipeline to an onshore geological storage site in Romania, establishing one of the first full CCS value chains in Eastern Europe. The project enables production of approximately 2 million tonnes per year of near-zero cement from 2032
Technology Type: End-of-pipe CO? capture combining pressure swing adsorption (PSA) and cryogenic separation to flue gases from cement and lime kilns, generating a high-purity CO? stream. The captured CO? is compressed and transported via pipeline to a retrofitted onshore geological storage site, where it is injected into subsurface formations for permanent sequestration. The system integrates multi-source capture, shared processing infrastructure, and a full capture–transport–storage chain.

Address: Botlekweg 121, HAVENNR 4060, 3197 KA Botlek Rotterdam, Netherlands
Short Description: CFC Pilot for CCS
Full Description: CFCPILOT4CCS is an Innovation Fund IF22 small-scale project led by Esso Nederland BV at the Rotterdam refinery. The project demonstrates carbonate fuel cell (CFC) technology for CO? capture from industrial flue gases, with a capture capacity of approximately 4.6 kt CO? per year. The system integrates carbon capture with electricity and hydrogen co-production, showcasing a novel approach to industrial decarbonisation at pilot scale.
Technology Type: The project deploys molten carbonate fuel cell (MCFC) technology for CO? capture, where flue gas is fed to the cathode side and CO? is electrochemically transported across the electrolyte to the anode. This process simultaneously concentrates CO?, generates electricity, and produces hydrogen-rich streams. The system operates at high temperature (~600–650°C) and enables capture efficiencies above 90% while integrating energy recovery. The captured CO? stream is conditioned for further handling, though permanent storage is not the primary objective of the pilot.

Short Description: Innovation in Carbon Capture and Transport
Full Description: CO2LLECT is a large-scale CCS project at Cemex’s Rüdersdorf cement plant in Germany, aiming to capture approximately 1.3 MtCO? per year using Linde’s advanced HISORP® adsorptive-cryogenic capture technology. The project will integrate a full CCS value chain, with captured CO? liquefied on site and initially transported by rail to a coastal hub before being shipped to offshore geological storage in the North Sea. It is one of the largest CCS projects in the cement sector and aims to enable carbon-neutral cement production at the site by around 2030
Technology Type: Adsorptive-cryogenic CO? capture using Linde’s HISORP® technology at a cement plant (~1.3 MtCO?/year), combined with on-site liquefaction, rail transport to a coastal CO? hub, and ship transport to offshore geological storage in the North Sea; designed as a full-chain CCS system for large-scale cement decarbonisation.

Short Description: CO?ncrEAT will capture CO? from Lhoist’s lime plant and use Carbstone® technology to produce carbon-negative precast blocks, storing more CO? than emitted over 10 years and enabling a circular economy.
Full Description: CO?ncrEAT will capture CO? from Lhoist’s lime plant and use Carbstone® technology to produce carbon-negative precast blocks, storing more CO? than emitted over 10 years and enabling a circular economy.
Technology Type: Accelerated carbonation mineralisation CCU: CO? reacted with lime and steel slag by-products to produce CO?-negative construction aggregates and building materials

Short Description: Coda by Carbfix - a highly scalable, cost effective CO2 mineral storage hub
Full Description: Coda Terminal is a CO? transport and storage project developed by Carbfix at Straumsvík in Hafnarfjörđur, Iceland. The project is designed as an onshore hub receiving captured CO? from industrial sources in Northern Europe via ship, followed by temporary storage and injection into nearby basalt formations. The system is designed to mineralise approximately 2.1 MtCO? per year, corresponding to 21.1 MtCO? over ten years of operation. The project was awarded funding under the EU Innovation Fund (IF21). The Straumsvík project was cancelled in early 2025 after local opposition, and alternative locations are being assessed.
Technology Type: The system integrates maritime CO? transport, onshore handling, and in-situ mineral storage. Liquefied CO? is shipped to a coastal terminal, unloaded into storage tanks, and injected via wells into basaltic formations. During injection, CO? is dissolved in water (freshwater or seawater) to form carbonated fluid, which reacts with calcium-, magnesium-, and iron-rich basalt to precipitate stable carbonate minerals. Mineralisation occurs rapidly, with high conversion efficiency approaching complete fixation, enabling permanent geological storage at scales exceeding 2 MtCO? per year.

Address: Spain-based maritime transport system (Mediterranean–North Sea linkage possible)
Short Description: Pioneering CO? Maritime Transport for CCS Across the Mediterranean
Full Description: COnet2 Sea is a maritime carbon dioxide transport project developed by SCALE GREEN ENERGY S.L. in Spain. The project establishes a large-scale shipping service for transporting liquefied CO? from industrial emitters, such as cement plants, to permanent geological storage sites in Europe. The system enables cross-border CO? transport between Mediterranean sources and storage locations, including potential connections to North Sea storage infrastructure, supporting the development of a CO? transport network for hard-to-abate industries.
Technology Type: Maritime CO? transport service: medium-pressure liquid CO? (LCO?) vessel with zeolite-based onboard CO? capture unit + bioLNG propulsion + innovative boil-off gas management using LNG cold; CO? shipped from cement plant to Mediterranean/North Sea geological storage

Address: Via Buttrio, 28, 33050 Pozzuolo del Friuli UD, Italy
Short Description: Carbon Capture and Use at a STeel plant with an Advanced solution to Reach Decarbonisation
Full Description: CUSTARD is a small-scale carbon capture and utilisation project developed by Acciaierie Bertoli Safau S.p.A. with Danieli & C. Officine Meccaniche S.p.A. at the ABS steel plant in Pozzuolo del Friuli (Cargnacco), Udine, Italy. The project captures approximately 13,000 tonnes of CO? per year from a steel reheating furnace and converts the captured CO? into sodium bicarbonate. The publicly stated greenhouse-gas avoidance is 179,040 tonnes CO?e over the first 10 years of operation, and the project fiche gives 31 March 2029 as the entry-into-operation date.
Technology Type: The process treats reheating-furnace flue gas through a flue-gas conditioning and CO? concentration unit, followed by a caustic-soda scrubbing step and a three-phase reactor in which captured CO? reacts to form sodium bicarbonate; downstream crystallisation and purification produce food-grade product. The system is designed to capture and utilise about 13 ktCO?/year, corresponding to up to 50% of CO? emissions from the targeted walking-beam reheating furnace, with no geological storage step

Short Description: Danube Onshore Fermentation Carbon Removals
Full Description: Danube Removals is a full-chain bioenergy carbon capture and storage (Bio-CCS) project located in Dunaföldvár, Hungary, developed by Danube Energy Ventures Kft. in partnership with ClonBio Group and EMOV. The project captures approximately 500,000 tonnes of biogenic CO? annually from fermentation processes at the Pannonia Bio biorefinery. The captured CO? is transported via pipeline and permanently stored in an onshore saline aquifer within the Pannonian Basin. The project generates certified carbon removal credits and represents the first onshore geological CO? storage project in the European Union, with total avoided emissions exceeding 5.6 million tonnes of CO? over the project period.
Technology Type: The project applies Bio-CCS by capturing high-purity biogenic CO? from fermentation and anaerobic digestion processes at a grain biorefinery. CO? is dehydrated, compressed, and transported via a short-distance pipeline (~10–15 km) to an onshore saline aquifer for permanent geological storage. The system operates at an annual scale of ~0.5 MtCO?, with near-complete capture due to the concentrated nature of fermentation streams, enabling low capture energy demand and high CO? purity suitable for direct compression and injection.

Address: ZI du clos pré, 27460 Alizay, France
Short Description: Decarbonation en Seine-Eure et sur la Zone industrielle de Rouen
Full Description: DEZiR is a large-scale synthetic sustainable aviation fuel project developed by Verso Energy in Normandy, with the fuel production site at Petit-Couronne and the biogenic CO? source at the BEA biomass boiler in Alizay. The project integrates capture and pipeline transfer of about 350,000 tonnes of biogenic CO? per year, hydrogen production by electrolysis, and synthesis steps leading to about 81,000 tonnes per year of e-SAF. The product is intended for connection into the regional fuel logistics system serving major Western European aviation markets.
Technology Type: The project captures biogenic CO? at the BEA biomass boiler in Alizay and transports it by pipeline to Petit-Couronne, where hydrogen is produced by water electrolysis using dedicated electricity supply. The CO? and hydrogen are converted to methanol in a methanolation step, and the methanol is then upgraded to jet-range hydrocarbons through a Methanol-to-Jet process to produce about 81 kt/year of e-SAF from roughly 350 kt/year of CO? feedstock.

Address: Via Vittorio Emanuele II, 419, 24033 Calusco d'Adda BG, Italy
Short Description: Decarbonisation of the Rezzato and Mazzano cement plant
Full Description: DREAM is a carbon capture and storage project developed by Heidelberg Materials Italia Cementi at the Rezzato-Mazzano cement plant in Rezzato, in the province of Brescia, Italy. The project covers both grey and white clinker production lines and is designed to capture around 1 MtCO? per year for permanent geological storage through the Ravenna CCS system
Technology Type: The project combines oxyfuel-advanced cryogenic capture on the grey clinker line with post-combustion amine capture on the white clinker line. Waste-heat recovery from the plant is used to supply thermal energy for solvent regeneration, and the captured CO? is processed into a high-purity stream suitable for pipeline transport to depleted gas reservoirs associated with the Ravenna CCS hub for permanent storage. Public sources indicate a capture rate above 95% of plant emissions and a capture volume of about 1 MtCO? per year.

Address: Herřya Industrial Park, Hydrovegen 55, 3936 Porsgrunn, Norway
Short Description: Innovative and cost-efficient production process for Power-to-Liquid using industrial off-gases
Full Description: Norwegian e-fuel pilot converting blast furnace gas into synthetic fuels via P2X, achieving >99% GHG reduction.
Technology Type: Power-to-Liquid e-fuels: post-combustion amine CO? capture from ferro/silicon-manganese plant; PEM electrolysis green H?; Fischer-Tropsch synthesis ? e-SAF/liquid fuels; 22 kt/yr CO? input; small refinery pilot

Short Description: electroMethanol-Rhône
Full Description: eM-Rhône is a French carbon capture and utilisation project led by Elyse Energy. It combines renewable hydrogen production on the Roches-Roussillon chemical platform in Salaise-sur-Sanne with carbon dioxide captured at Holcim/Lafarge’s cement plant in Le Teil, Ardčche, and transported by rail to the methanol site. The project is designed around 170 MW of PEM electrolysis, about 27 kt/year of hydrogen production, and about 195 kt/year of CO? use to produce large-scale e-methanol for chemical and maritime markets.
Technology Type: The project captures concentrated CO? from the Le Teil cement plant using a fully electric cryogenic capture scheme, liquefies and transports the CO? by rail to Salaise-sur-Sanne, then receives, stores, regasifies and purifies the CO? before reaction with hydrogen produced on site by 170 MW PEM electrolysis. The hydrogen is compressed and purified, then combined with the purified CO? in a methanol synthesis unit, followed by intermediate storage and final methanol storage via the adjacent TEPSA logistics system. The updated Innovation Fund factsheet gives 195 kt/year CO? input, 27 kt/year hydrogen production and 138 kt/year methanol output; Elyse’s project page still states 150 kt/year methanol.

Short Description: Arcadia eFuels,  decarbonizing aviation
Full Description: ENDOR is a large-scale Power-to-Liquid e-fuels project developed by Arcadia eFuels Vordingborg ApS at Vordingborg Harbour on Masnedř in Denmark. The facility is permitted at Belgiensvej 5, 4760 Vordingborg and is designed to produce synthetic aviation fuel as e-kerosene/eSAF, with e-naphtha as a secondary product. Verified permitting documents indicate annual output of about 70.9 kt of e-kerosene and 15.3 kt of e-naphtha, equivalent to about 107,800 mł of e-fuels
Technology Type: The project uses renewable electricity to produce hydrogen by electrolysis, combines hydrogen with sustainable CO? to form syngas, and converts that syngas through Fischer–Tropsch synthesis into long-chain hydrocarbons that are upgraded into e-kerosene/eSAF, with e-naphtha and e-diesel as co-products. The permitted plant is designed for about 360 MW power input, about 92% annual operating time, seawater intake and desalination integrated with waste-heat use, and annual production of about 70.9 kt e-kerosene and 15.3 kt e-naphtha

Short Description: Improved calcination and carbon capture for the largest lime plant in Europe
Full Description: EVEREST is a carbon capture and storage project for the Rheinkalk Werk Flandersbach lime plant in Wülfrath, Germany, developed by Rheinkalk GmbH, part of Lhoist. The project combines retrofit capture on existing lime kilns with new oxyfuel kiln capacity and links the plant to CO? transport and offshore geological storage. Official project data show an Innovation Fund grant of EUR 228.7 million, an expected GHG emissions avoidance of 9.309295 MtCO?e over the first ten years of operation, a start date of 1 January 2024, and entry into operation on 30 September 2031.
Technology Type: The project integrates two capture routes at the Flandersbach lime plant: a dedicated flue-gas CO? capture unit connected to existing rotary kilns and parallel flow regenerating kilns, and three new oxyfuel PFR kilns designed to produce a high-CO? process stream. Air Liquide’s Cryocap technology is referenced by Lhoist for capture, concentration, purification and compression, followed by liquefaction and transport through a chain including pipeline, rail and/or ship to offshore geological storage in the North Sea. The project is designed around about 1.5 MtCO? per year captured and stored, with official GHG avoidance of 9.309295 MtCO?e over the first ten years of operation

Short Description: First German inland cement plant Geseke becomes net carbon negative by implementing a full CCS chain
Full Description: GeZero is a large-scale CCS project at Heidelberg Materials’ cement plant in Geseke, Germany, designed to capture approximately 700,000 tonnes of CO? per year using a second-generation oxyfuel kiln combined with cryogenic CO? purification. The project aims to establish a full CCS value chain for inland industrial sites, including on-site capture, liquefaction, and rail-based transport to a CO? hub, followed by shipment to offshore storage in the North Sea. It represents one of the first attempts to deploy CCS at scale in a landlocked cement facility in Europe.
Technology Type: Oxyfuel-based carbon capture using a second-generation oxyfuel kiln combined with cryogenic CO? purification (~0.7 MtCO?/year), with interim storage and rail transport to a CO? hub and onward shipment to offshore geological storage in the North Sea.

Short Description: KUJAWY GO4ECOPLANET
Full Description: GO4ECOPlanet is a large-scale CCS project at Holcim’s Kujawy cement plant in Poland, aiming to capture approximately 1 MtCO? per year using Air Liquide’s Cryocap™ FG cryogenic capture technology. The project is designed to enable near-complete decarbonisation of cement production by combining carbon capture with circular production processes and alternative raw materials. Captured CO? will be liquefied and transported to a coastal terminal for shipment to offshore geological storage, forming part of a broader CCS value chain.
Technology Type: Air Liquide CryocapTM FG (high-pressure cryogenic CO? separation from cement flue gas, 99.9% purity) at Holcim Kujawy cement plant; CO? liquefied; rail transport to Gda?sk port; ship to North Sea geological storage; 1 Mt/yr

Short Description: Towards a carbon negative large-scale clinker plant through first-ever demonstration of a groundbreaking flue gas recirculation & concentration-based concept paired with a full CCS solution
Full Description: GO4ZERO is a cement decarbonisation project developed by Holcim Belgique SA at the Obourg cement plant in Wallonia, Belgium. The project combines a new air-oxyfuel switchable clinker production line with carbon capture, purification, pipeline transport, liquefaction and offshore geological storage. Public project documentation states an annual CO? capture volume of about 1.1 Mt and total avoided greenhouse gas emissions of 10.045932 MtCO?e over the project period.
Technology Type: The project replaces existing clinker production with an air-oxyfuel switchable kiln that increases CO? concentration in the flue gas, followed by Air Liquide Cryocap™ OXY carbon purification/capture. The purified CO? stream is transported by pipeline through the Belgian CO? network toward an export hub such as Antwerp@C, where it is liquefied and transferred to ships for permanent offshore geological storage in the North Sea. The published capture scale is about 1.1 MtCO? per year, with project entry into operation planned for the end of 2028.

Short Description: Green Methanol in Galicia
Full Description: GREEN MEIGA is a large-scale e-methanol project led by Iberdrola Clientes with FORESA participation in Begonte, Lugo, Galicia, Spain. The project combines 151 MW of electrolysis with captured CO? to produce 100,000 tonnes per year of e-methanol. Public project materials indicate around 20,000 tonnes per year of renewable hydrogen production, about 152,698 tonnes per year of CO? use, and total avoided greenhouse-gas emissions of 2.901 million tonnes CO?e over the first ten years of operation
Technology Type: The project integrates a hybrid renewable-hydrogen block based on alkaline, PEM, SOEC and co-SOEC electrolysis with a CO? supply chain that combines advanced capture approaches, including enzyme-based capture and direct air capture, together with biogenic CO? handling described by Iberdrola and Spanish public sources; the hydrogen and conditioned CO? are fed to an integrated methanol synthesis plant designed for 100,000 t/yr e-methanol output, with about 152,698 t/yr CO? input and around 151 MW electrolysis capacity.

Short Description: Contributing to EU’s CO2 emissions reduction target by establishing critical EU storage capacity
Full Description: Greensand Future is a large-scale CO? transport and storage project in Denmark led by INEOS, aiming to establish the European Union’s first operational offshore CO? storage facility. The project will transport liquefied CO? from industrial emitters to a terminal at the Port of Esbjerg, from where it will be shipped to the offshore Nini West depleted oil field in the Danish North Sea for permanent geological storage. Initial operations are expected to begin around 2025–2026 with a storage capacity of approximately 0.4 MtCO? per year, with plans to scale significantly in later phases.
Technology Type: CO? transport and storage infrastructure involving collection of liquefied CO? from industrial emitters, temporary storage and handling at the Port of Esbjerg terminal, ship transport to offshore facilities, and injection into the depleted Nini West oil reservoir in the Danish North Sea for permanent geological storage; scalable system designed to expand from ~0.4 MtCO?/year to multi-megaton capacity.

Short Description: The first 1GW low-carbon H2 production facility to enable and accelerate industrial decarbonisation, critical H2/CO2 infrastructure realisation and H2 market development in Northern Europe.
Full Description: H2BE is a low-carbon hydrogen project developed by Electrabel and Equinor at the Rodenhuize industrial site in Desteldonk, Ghent, Belgium. The project comprises a 1 GW hydrogen production facility based on autothermal reforming with integrated carbon capture, designed to supply more than 210,000 tonnes of hydrogen per year. Captured CO? is transported for permanent geological storage on the Norwegian continental shelf
Technology Type: Natural gas is converted to hydrogen in a 1 GW autothermal reforming plant at the Rodenhuize site. The process includes integrated point-source carbon capture with a capture rate above 95% during hydrogen production, after which the captured CO? is conditioned for transport and sent to permanent offshore geological storage on the Norwegian continental shelf; the facility is designed to produce more than 210 kt H? per year.

Short Description: HUb for Carbon Capture and Storage to decarbonise the hard-to Abate Regions in Poland — HuCCSar
Full Description: HuCCSar is an onshore CCS demonstration in Poland led by Góra?d?e Cement S.A., part of Heidelberg Materials Polska, with Schlumberger Poland. The project is linked to the Góra?d?e cement plant and develops a domestic CO? hub, pipeline and underground onshore storage system in Poland. Public project factsheets describe storage capacity above 17,000 tonnes of CO? per year and permanent storage of 51,711 tonnes of CO? over the course of the project
Technology Type: HuCCSar integrates a domestic CCS chain consisting of captured industrial CO? from the Góra?d?e cement value chain, transport through a purpose-built pipeline system, injection into modular onshore underground storage facilities in Poland, and advanced Monitoring, Measurement and Verification systems providing high-resolution, real-time monitoring and control; the pilot is designed for storage capacity above 17 ktCO? per year and 51.7 ktCO? permanent storage over the project period

Address: 80 km of Oinois - Magoulas Provincial Road, 19 600 Magoula, (PO Box 18)
Short Description: IFESTOS – one of the largest carbon capture projects in Europe to enable the production of zero carbon cement & concrete and create decarbonization synergies with regional industries
Full Description: IFESTOS is a large-scale CCS project at TITAN’s Kamari cement plant in Greece, near Athens, designed to establish one of Europe’s largest cement carbon capture value chains. The project will retrofit the plant with an innovative capture configuration combining first- and second-generation oxyfuel technologies with cryogenic capture to collect approximately 1.9 MtCO? per year. Captured CO? will be liquefied and transported to permanent geological storage in the Mediterranean.
Technology Type: Hybrid oxyfuel + cryogenic carbon capture at the TITAN Kamari cement plant, combining first- and second-generation oxyfuel technologies with cryogenic capture to achieve about 1.9 MtCO?/year capture; captured CO? is liquefied and intended for transport to permanent geological storage in the Mediterranean as part of a large CCS value chain.

Short Description: Innovation for Zero Emissions in Helsingborg
Full Description: INNOZHERO is a waste-to-energy carbon capture project at the Filbornaverket CHP plant in Helsingborg, Sweden, developed by Öresundskraft. The project will capture approximately 0.2 MtCO? per year from mixed fossil and biogenic waste streams, enabling both emissions reduction and partial carbon removal. The captured CO? will be liquefied and transported via truck and ship to offshore geological storage in the Danish North Sea (Greensand project), one of Europe’s emerging operational storage sites. The project is in an advanced development phase, with operation targeted for 2028.
Technology Type: Aker Carbon Capture Just Catch™ post-combustion amine capture at Öresundskraft Filbornaverket WtE CHP plant, Helsingborg. CO? transported by truck/ship to INEOS Greensand Nini West offshore reservoir, Danish North Sea.

Address: 71st km Athens-Corinth National Road, 20003 Agioi Theodoroi, Greece
Short Description: Innovative low caRbon hydrogen and methanol productIon by large Scale carbon capture
Full Description: IRIS is a refinery decarbonisation project developed by Motor Oil at the Agioi Theodoroi refinery in Greece. It captures CO? from the steam methane reformer flue gas to produce ultra-low-carbon hydrogen for refinery and transport uses, while a smaller share of captured CO? is combined with renewable hydrogen to produce e-methanol. The project is designed for about 0.85 MtCO? per year of capture and 8.585 MtCO?e of avoided emissions over the first ten years of operation.
Technology Type: The project applies post-combustion CO? capture to the refinery steam methane reformer flue gas, with an official capture rate of 95%. Most of the captured CO? is directed to offshore geological storage in the North Aegean Sea, while a small fraction is routed with renewable hydrogen to an e-methanol synthesis unit producing about 10,000 t/yr of e-methanol. The integrated system is designed around approximately 0.85 MtCO?/yr capture

Site Owner: BASF Antwerpen + Air Liquide Antwerpen/Antwerp site
Short Description: Kairos-at-C, Building strong momentum for massive decarbonisation in the EU through a unique end to end CCS project
Full Description: Kairos@C is a large-scale CCS project in the Port of Antwerp developed by Air Liquide and BASF to capture CO? from hydrogen, ammonia and ethylene oxide production units on the Zandvliet industrial platform. The project includes capture, pipeline transport within the port, liquefaction and export, followed by ship transport to permanent North Sea geological storage. It is designed to avoid about 14 MtCO?e over the first ten years of operation.
Technology Type: Kairos@C integrates Cryocap™ cryogenic CO? capture and purification across five industrial sources—two hydrogen plants, two ethylene oxide plants, and one ammonia plant—on the Antwerp Zandvliet platform. Captured CO? is transported by pipeline to a large-scale port liquefaction and export terminal, loaded onto ships, and sent to permanent geological storage sites in the North Sea. The system is designed at about 1.4 MtCO?/yr and is scalable in phases

Address: Forsbacka, Gävle Municipality, Gävleborg County, Sweden
Short Description: The Plagazi Process, A Patented Plasma Gasification System to Turn Hard-to-Recycle Waste into Clean Hydrogen
Full Description: The IF23 pilot project is a waste-to-hydrogen project developed by Plagazi in Sweden through the beneficiary Köping Hydrogen Park AB and now publicly presented as Gävle Circular Park in Forsbacka, near Gävle. It converts non-recyclable waste into hydrogen through plasma gasification and captures the process CO? for storage or utilisation. The official pilot phase is described at up to 4,000 t/yr of hydrogen from 22,000 t/yr of waste, while Plagazi’s later public site plan describes a larger two-phase commercial build-out in Forsbacka.
Technology Type: The process uses high-temperature plasma arc gasification to convert non-recyclable waste into syngas, followed by hydrogen recovery and CO? capture to produce sustainable hydrogen with a net-negative or very low-carbon profile. The official IF23 pilot phase is specified at 22,000 t/yr waste input and up to 4,000 t/yr hydrogen, while later public site planning for Gävle Circular Park indicates a larger phased configuration with capture of liquid CO? for storage or utilisation and surplus heat export to district heating.

Short Description: KOdeCO net zero
Full Description: KOdeCO is a carbon capture project at Holcim’s Koroma?no cement plant in Croatia, aiming to capture approximately 3.7 MtCO2e. The project will retrofit the plant with cryogenic CO? capture technology and is designed to achieve net-zero cement production. The captured CO? is planned to be liquefied and transported by ship to offshore storage sites in the Mediterranean, although the full transport and storage chain is not yet finalised.
Technology Type: The project uses Air Liquide’s Cryocap™ FG technology, a cryogenic CO? capture process that separates and liquefies CO? directly from flue gas. This approach integrates capture and purification in a single system and avoids the use of conventional solvent-based (amine) processes.. Liquefied CO? loaded onto vessels and shipped to first permanent offshore geological storage in the Mediterranean Sea.

Full Description: LaunchStores4AramisCCS is a large-scale offshore CO? storage project in the Netherlands led by TotalEnergies, forming part of the Aramis CCS value chain. The project focuses on developing two offshore storage sites (L4A and K14) in depleted gas fields under the North Sea, enabling the permanent storage of CO? from industrial emitters. CO? will be transported via the Aramis pipeline network from a central hub at the Maasvlakte in the Port of Rotterdam to offshore injection platforms. The project is designed to store up to 5 MtCO? per year and is expected to avoid over 30 MtCO? in its first ten years of operation.
Technology Type: Offshore geological CO? storage system in depleted gas fields (L4A and K14) involving transport of compressed CO? via pipeline from an onshore hub to offshore injection platforms, followed by injection at depths of 3–4 km below the North Sea seabed; incorporates Friction-Controlled Dual-Phase (FCDP) injection technology to enable stable and efficient CO? storage at scale (~5 MtCO?/year).

Short Description: LimE OPeration decARboniseD
Full Description: Leopard is a Carmeuse project at the Aisemont lime plant in Fosses-la-Ville, Belgium, to decarbonise more than 80,000 tonnes per year of lime production. It combines bioenergy with carbon capture and storage and is expected to avoid 788,439 tonnes CO?e over its first ten years of operation.
Technology Type: The project retrofits a vertical lime kiln with a hybrid capture configuration combining kiln-process modification for CO? preconcentration and a membrane-based, fully electrified carbon-capture step. Bioenergy is used as the kiln’s main fuel source, and the captured CO? is intended for transport and permanent geological storage via planned Belgian/Northwest European CCS infrastructure.

Short Description: Biocarbon reductants for decarbonising the metallurgical industry
Full Description: MetZero is a Norwegian industrial pilot project led by NG Nordic AS, with Cruda AS and other partners, to produce biocarbon from wood waste and low-value biomass for use as a fossil reductant substitute in metallurgical production. Public project information places the facility in the Řra industrial cluster in Fredrikstad, and the 2026 CINEA fiche reports 414,520 tonnes CO?e avoided over the first ten years of operation.
Technology Type: The project converts waste wood and low-value biomass into biocarbon for metallurgical use through pyrolysis followed by agglomeration, producing a biogenic reductant with high carbon density and reactivity suitable for silicon and manganese production. The public fiche states output of 10 kt/year of biocarbon and 36 GWh of carbon-neutral energy, with no direct CO? capture, transport or storage step disclosed.

Short Description: Ascending to the top of CO2 avoidance in the EU cement sector through the innovative OxyCalciner technology
Full Description: OLYMPUS is a large-scale CCS project at Holcim’s Milaki cement plant in Evia, Greece, designed to capture approximately 1 MtCO? per year using a hybrid system combining OxyCalciner technology and Air Liquide’s Cryocap™ FG cryogenic capture. The project will retrofit the existing cement plant to achieve around 95% emissions reduction and produce near-zero cement at industrial scale. Captured CO? will be liquefied and transported by ship to offshore geological storage at the Prinos site in the Aegean Sea. Construction officially started in May 2025, with operation expected by 2029.
Technology Type: Hybrid oxyfuel-based carbon capture using OxyCalciner combined with Cryocap™ FG cryogenic purification (~1 MtCO?/year), with CO? liquefaction and ship transport to offshore geological storage at the Prinos site in the Aegean Sea.

Address: Pärnu Airport area, Pärnu County, Estonia
Short Description: Pärnu P2X Hub
Full Description: PP2XH (Pärnu P2X Hub) is a medium-scale renewable e-methanol and hydrogen project developed by MBH Power Energy (S) Pte. Ltd. with Pärnu P2X Hub OÜ in Pärnu Airport, Eametsa village, Tori municipality, Estonia. The project integrates on-site and contracted renewable electricity with hydrogen production and e-methanol synthesis, using biogenic CO? from local biomethane plants as carbon feedstock. Public 2025–2026 sources indicate annual production of about 10,000 tonnes of e-methanol and 2,000 tonnes of renewable hydrogen, with expected GHG emissions avoidance of about 155,063 tCO?e.
Technology Type: The project combines renewable electricity supply, a smart microgrid, a 25 MW alkaline electrolyser, hydrogen compression and storage, and a methanol synthesis unit that reacts renewable hydrogen with captured biogenic CO? from local biomethane plants to produce about 10,000 t/y e-methanol, while also supplying about 2,000 t/y renewable hydrogen for transport applications. The power system described in 2025 sources includes 51 MWp solar, 18 MW wind under PPA, and a 10 MW renewable grid connection for backup and balancing

Facility Type: Chemical production / sustainable methanol CCU plant integrated at existing Perstorp Stenungsund chemicals site.
Short Description: Production of sustainable methanol as raw material for chemical products by first-of-a-kind Carbon Capture and Utilization process integrated with world scale electrolysis unit
Full Description: Project Air is a large-scale Innovation Fund project led by Perstorp Oxo AB at Perstorp’s Stenungsund chemicals site in Sweden. The project integrates carbon capture and utilisation with renewable hydrogen production to manufacture 200,000 tonnes of sustainable methanol per year for use as a chemical feedstock, replacing fossil methanol in Perstorp’s European operations. The Innovation Fund factsheet reports 4.06 MtCO?e of greenhouse-gas avoidance over ten years, and the project is planned to enter operation in 2027.
Technology Type: CO? is captured from Perstorp’s existing chemical production processes (industrial point source) and combined with renewable hydrogen produced via a large-scale electrolysis unit powered by renewable electricity, along with biomethane and residue streams, to produce synthesis gas that is converted into methanol via catalytic synthesis; this represents a large-scale CCU Power-to-X system where CO? is utilised as a feedstock for sustainable methanol production (~200 kt methanol/year).
Status: Project development / basic engineering; financing secured and environmental permit granted.

Short Description: Renewable Energy Cluster for Agro- Industrial Methanol Production
Full Description: RECLAIM is a BioCirc project at the Vesthimmerland biogas plant in Farsř, Denmark, converting an existing biogas site into an integrated facility producing RFNBO e-methanol, biomethane and digestate-based fertilizer from agricultural biomass residues and renewable electricity. The official project factsheet states planned annual output of 16,000 tonnes of e-methanol, 22.7 million Nmł of biomethane and 760,000 tonnes of digestate fertilizer.
Technology Type: Anaerobic digestion of agricultural residues produces biogas upgraded to biomethane, with separated biogenic CO? combined with renewable hydrogen from electrolysis to synthesise RFNBO methanol; digestate is recovered as fertiliser. Output: 16,000 t/yr methanol, 22.7 million Nmł/yr biomethane, 760,000 t/yr digestate; 670,780 tCO?e avoided over 10 years.
Status: Grant signed; under development / advancing toward FID

Sector: Manufacturing of components for renewable energy
Facility Type: manufacturing scale-up of reversible biogas power plants / renewable-energy components.
Short Description: Revolutionizing Energy, scaling manufacturing of innovative, reversible, highly efficient, carbon-negative power plants
Full Description: RESILIENCE is a manufacturing scale-up project developed by Reverion GmbH in Eresing, Germany, focused on expanding production of modular reversible power plants for biogas applications. The project adds production, assembly and factory testing capacity for rSOC-based units that operate with electrical efficiency of up to 80% and are designed for deployment at biogas plants. Public Innovation Fund documentation reports 2.407 MtCO?e of expected avoided emissions by 2035.
Technology Type: The technology is a containerised reversible Solid Oxide Cell (rSOC) system that operates in fuel-cell mode to convert biogas into electricity at up to 80% electrical efficiency and in electrolysis mode to convert electricity into hydrogen-based energy carriers. During biogas conversion, the process separates a concentrated CO? stream that is suitable for utilisation or geological storage. The RESILIENCE project scales manufacturing of these units through expanded component preparation, specialised module assembly, final assembly and factory acceptance testing at Reverion’s Eresing site.

Site Owner: RYAM for the capture site; e-fuel production site not yet publicly finalised
Address: 1154 Av. du Général Leclerc, 40400 Tartas, France
Short Description: Renewable e-SAF Tartas
Full Description: ReSTart is a large-scale Power-to-Liquid sustainable aviation fuel project developed by Verso Energy in Tartas and Bégaar, Landes, France. The project integrates capture of 334,000 t/yr of biogenic CO? from RYAM’s Tartas cellulose site with on-site production of 48,000 t/yr of renewable hydrogen, conversion to 223,000 t/yr of e-methanol, and upgrading to 81,000 t/yr of e-SAF for supply to major Western European aviation markets
Technology Type: Biogenic CO? emitted from RYAM’s biomass boilers at Tartas is captured and combined with hydrogen produced by water electrolysis using a dedicated high-voltage power connection of at least 380 MW. The CO? and H? are synthesised into e-methanol, which is then upgraded through a methanol-to-jet (MTJ) route, with Honeywell’s eFining technology selected for Verso Energy’s e-SAF platform. Public project material states design capacities of 334 kt/yr CO? captured and utilised, 48 kt/yr H?, 223 kt/yr e-methanol, and 81 kt/yr e-SAF

Short Description: Replacing gray hydrogen with green and blue hydrogen
Full Description: SHARC will decarbonise Neste’s Porvoo refinery by replacing grey hydrogen with green and blue hydrogen and integrating CCS to capture and store CO? offshore.
Technology Type: Post-combustion amine scrubbing CO? capture at Neste Porvoo refinery (fossil + biogenic CO?); liquid CO? ship transport to North Sea geological storage; green + blue H? co-production; 0.4 Mt/yr

Short Description: Silverstone: Full-scale CO2 capture and mineral storage at the Hellisheidi power station
Full Description: Silverstone is a carbon capture and geological storage project developed by Carbfix in cooperation with ON Power at the Hellisheiđi geothermal power plant in Iceland. The project captures CO? from geothermal power generation, dissolves it in water, and stores it underground in basalt through mineralisation. Public project sources describe total annual capture and storage at about 34,000 tCO? after scale-up, with around 150,000 tonnes of CO? stored over the project’s operational phase, while the updated European Commission factsheet reports 172,415 tCO?e of expected GHG emissions avoidance.
Technology Type: Carbfix mineralisation: CO? absorbed from geothermal plant exhaust dissolved in water; injected into basalt rock; permanently mineralised to carbonate within 2 years.

Site Owner: Eramet Norway AS / LanzaTech NZ Inc
Facility Type: Industrial CCUS pilot at manganese smelter
Short Description: Sustainable, Low- carbon Advancements in LanzaFlex Operations for Manufacturing
Full Description: SLALOM 2.0 is a pilot CCUS project developed by LanzaTech with Eramet Norway at Herřya Industrial Park in Porsgrunn, Norway. The project converts carbon-monoxide-rich off-gases from Eramet’s manganese smelter into ethanol and produces a concentrated CO? stream for liquefaction and permanent geological storage in the North Sea. Publicly disclosed outputs include 23.5 kt/year of ethanol and 126 kt/year of liquefied CO?, with total greenhouse-gas avoidance of 1,698,175 tCO?e over the first ten years.
Technology Type: The project applies LanzaTech’s gas-fermentation process to CO-rich industrial off-gases from manganese alloy production, using a higher-efficiency bioreactor and engineered microbes to convert part of the carbon into ethanol. The integrated plant also purifies and liquefies the concentrated CO? tail gas stream for transport and permanent geological storage in the North Sea. The disclosed performance is 23.5 kt/year ethanol output, 126 kt/year liquefied CO?, 27% of input carbon converted into fermentation products, 69% permanently stored, and 97% of carbon sent to the project captured within the integrated CCUS configuration.

Address: Approximately 135 km offshore in southwest of Stavanger
Full Description: STARFISH is a large-scale offshore CO? storage project, part of the Havstjerne carbon storage development, led by Harbour Energy in partnership with Stella Maris CCS. The project will establish a novel CO? storage solution based on ship transport and direct offshore injection, eliminating the need for fixed pipeline infrastructure. CO? from multiple industrial sources will be transported by specialised carrier vessels and injected directly into a deep saline aquifer in the Havstjerne reservoir via a floating injection unit. The project is designed to store approximately 42.75 MtCO? over its first ten years of operation and to provide open-access storage services across Europe.
Technology Type: Offshore geological CO? storage system using ship-based transport and direct injection via a floating direct injection unit (FDIU), enabling CO? carrier vessels to offload and inject CO? directly into a deep saline aquifer (Havstjerne reservoir) without fixed pipeline infrastructure; open-access storage model with ~4.3 MtCO?/year capacity.

Site Owner: TAKE KAIR France / Hynamics-led project company
Address: Nantes Saint-Nazaire Port area, Donges (Loire-Atlantique), France
Short Description: Take Kair
Full Description: TAKE KAIR is a French pilot-scale Power-to-Liquid e-fuel project led by Hynamics to produce synthetic aviation fuel and e-naphtha at Donges in the Nantes Saint-Nazaire port area. The project uses renewable and low-carbon hydrogen together with about 160,000 tonnes per year of biogenic CO? sourced from the Saint-Pierre-la-Cour cement plant, and is designed to produce about 39,000 tonnes per year of e-kerosene and 11,000 tonnes per year of e-naphtha
Technology Type: The project captures and certifies a biogenic CO? stream from the Saint-Pierre-la-Cour cement plant, transports that CO? to the Donges e-fuel plant, produces renewable/low-carbon hydrogen by water electrolysis, converts CO? and H? to syngas via reverse water-gas shift, then synthesises liquid hydrocarbons through Fischer–Tropsch followed by refining into e-kerosene and e-naphtha; disclosed plant inputs and outputs include about 160 ktCO?/yr feedstock, around 300 MW grid connection, about 1,700 GWh/yr electricity use, and output of roughly 39 kt/yr e-kerosene plus 11 kt/yr e-naphtha

Address: Offshore storage site, ~45 km off Tarragona, Spain
Short Description: The reduction of CO2 emissions through geological storage, a catalyst of the CO2 market
Full Description: TarraCO2 is a large-scale offshore CO? storage project led by Repsol Exploración in Spain. The project is designed to provide about 2 million tonnes per year of permanent offshore CO? storage capacity for hard-to-abate industrial emitters in the Tarragona area, with a total storage capacity of 54 million tonnes. The storage site is publicly described as being around 45 km offshore of Tarragona
Technology Type: TarraCO2 receives captured CO? from industrial sources in the Tarragona region, transports the CO? to an offshore storage site, and injects it for permanent geological storage in a subsea reservoir system; the publicly disclosed scale is about 2 MtCO?/yr injection and 54 Mt total storage capacity, while the project factsheet positions it as offshore geological storage infrastructure serving the petrochemical, chemical, cement, and other hard-to-abate industries in north-eastern Spain
Status: Grant signed; pre-FID development and permitting

Address: 140 km north of Athens in the Livanates region, Greece
Short Description: ?hermo-lysi SA End-of-Life Tyres (ELTs) recycling facility utilizing innovative made- in-Europe Pyrolysis technology
Full Description: The Thermolysis project, developed by Thermo Lysi S.A., is located in Livanates, Greece, and consists of a large-scale facility for recycling end-of-life tyres (ELTs) using pyrolysis technology. The plant processes up to approximately 45,000 tonnes of tyres per year into recovered carbon black, pyrolysis oil, and steel. The system converts waste tyres into secondary raw materials for industrial reuse while reducing landfill and incineration. The project is supported by the EU Innovation Fund and is implemented with Pyrum Innovations AG as the technology provider.
Technology Type: The process uses thermolysis (pyrolysis) of end-of-life tyres in an oxygen-free reactor system, where rubber is decomposed at elevated temperatures into solid recovered carbon black, liquid hydrocarbons (pyrolysis oil), and non-condensable syngas. The plant integrates multiple modular reactors (up to six units) with a total processing capacity of approximately 45 kt/year of tyres. The produced syngas is internally recycled to supply around 60% of the plant’s energy demand, improving energy efficiency. Outputs are upgraded and reused as industrial feedstocks, substituting fossil-derived materials.

Address: Rúa Promontorio, s/n, 15620 Mugardos, A Coruńa, Spain
Facility Type: E-methanol plant integrated with existing industrial/CHP site
Short Description: Green Methanol manufacturing from CO2
Full Description: TRISKELION is an e-methanol project developed by Forestal del Atlántico at Mugardos, A Coruńa, Spain. The plant captures about 56,000 tonnes of CO? per year from CHP flue gas and combines it with renewable hydrogen from electrolysis to produce about 40,000 tonnes per year of e-methanol. The project is part of the EU Innovation Fund large-scale portfolio.
Technology Type: CCU e-methanol: biogenic CO? captured from Forestal del Atlántico forestry operations + renewable H? electrolysis + RFNBO methanol synthesis (MeOH); methanol for maritime transport decarbonisation; 56 kt/yr CO? input

Address: Route de la Cimenterie, 38390 Montalieu-Vercieu, France
Short Description: Vicat Advanced Industrial Alliance
Full Description: VAIA is a cement CCS project developed by Vicat at its Montalieu-Vercieu cement plant in Isčre, France. The project is designed to capture and permanently store about 1.2 MtCO? per year from cement production. Captured CO? is transported by pipeline along the Rhône Valley to the Fos Tonkin terminal at Fos-sur-Mer for liquefaction and shipment to a depleted gas reservoir in Italy for geological storage
Technology Type: Post-combustion CO? capture (electromechanical activation + electrified amine/post-combustion + heat integration) at Vicat Montalieu-Vercieu cement plant; CO? via repurposed SPESE oil pipeline ? Lacq/Elengy terminal ? geological storage in Italy (depleted gas); 1.2 Mt/yr
Status: Grant awarded / development and permitting / pre-FID

Short Description: Viken Industrial Pyrolysis for Emissions Reduction
Full Description: VIPER is a pyrolysis biorefinery project developed by Vow Green Metals AS at Viken Park in Fredrikstad, Norway. The facility converts woody biomass residues into biocarbon, biochar and bio-oil, with a planned production capacity of approximately 30,000 tonnes per year of biocarbon for metallurgical applications. The project achieves greenhouse gas emission reductions of around 100 ktCO? per year by substituting fossil-based reducing agents and materials in industrial value chains.
Technology Type: Slow pyrolysis biorefinery (Vow Green Metals technology): woody biomass by-products ? biocarbon (metallurgical reducing agent replacing fossil coke) + biochar (soil amendment) + bio-oil (bitumen replacement in asphalt); circular bioeconomy; GHG reduction via feedstock substitution
